title: The Romance Of Britomarte
content:
As related by Sergeant Leigh on the night he got his captaincy at the Restoration.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll tell you a story; but pass the ï؟½jackï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And let us make merry to-night, my men.
آ آ آ آ Aye, those were the days when my beard was black,
آ آ آ آ I like to remember them now and then,
آ آ آ آ Then Miles was living, and Cuthbert there,
آ آ آ آ On his lip was never a sign of down;
آ آ آ آ But I carry about some braided hair,
آ آ آ آ That has not yet changed from the glossy brown
آ آ آ آ That it showed the day when I broke the heart
آ آ آ آ Of that bravest of destriers, ï؟½Britomarteï؟½.

آ آ آ آ Sir Hugh was slain (may his soul find grace!)
آ آ آ آ In the fray that was neither lost nor won
آ آ آ آ At Edgehill, then to St. Hubertï؟½s Chase
آ آ آ آ Lord Goring despatched a garrison,
آ آ آ آ But men and horses were ill to spare,
آ آ آ آ And ere long the soldiers were shifted fast.
آ آ آ آ As for me, I never was quartered there
آ آ آ آ Till Marston Moor had been lost; at last,
آ آ آ آ As luck would have it, alone, and late
آ آ آ آ In the night, I rode to the northern gate.

آ آ آ آ I thought, as I passed through the moonlit park,
آ آ آ آ On the boyish days I used to spend
آ آ آ آ In the halls of the knight lying stiff and stark,
آ آ آ آ Thought on his lady, my fatherï؟½s friend
آ آ آ آ (Mine, too, in spite of my sinister bar,
آ آ آ آ But with that my story has naught to do),
آ آ آ آ She died the winter before the war,
آ آ آ آ Died giving birth to the baby Hugh.
آ آ آ آ He passï؟½d ere the green leaves clothed the bough,
آ آ آ آ And the orphan girl was the heiress now.

آ آ آ آ When I was a rude and a reckless boy,
آ آ آ آ And she a brave and a beautiful child,
آ آ آ آ I was her page, her playmate, her toy,
آ آ آ آ I have crownï؟½d her hair with the field-flowers wild,
آ آ آ آ Cowslip and crow-foot and coltï؟½s-foot bright,
آ آ آ آ I have carried her miles when the woods were wet,
آ آ آ آ I have read her romances of dame and knight;
آ آ آ آ She was my princess, my pride, my pet,
آ آ آ آ There was then this proverb us twain between,
آ آ آ آ For the glory of God and of Gwendoline.

آ آ آ آ She had grown to a maiden wonderful fair,
آ آ آ آ But for years I had scarcely seen her face.
آ آ آ آ Now, with troopers Holdsworth, Huntly, and Clare,
آ آ آ آ Old Miles kept guard at St. Hubertï؟½s Chase,
آ آ آ آ And the chatelaine was a Mistress Ruth,
آ آ آ آ Sir Hughï؟½s half-sister, an ancient dame,
آ آ آ آ But a mettlesome soul had she forsooth,
آ آ آ آ As she showï؟½d when the time of her trial came.
آ آ آ آ I bore despatches to Miles and to her,
آ آ آ آ To warn them against the bands of Kerr.

آ آ آ آ And mine would have been a perilous ride
آ آ آ آ With the rebel horsemen, we knew not where
آ آ آ آ They were scattered over that country side,
آ آ آ آ If it had not been for my brave brown mare.
آ آ آ آ She was iron-sinewï؟½d and satin-skinnï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Ribbï؟½d like a drum and limbï؟½d like a deer,
آ آ آ آ Fierce as the fire and fleet as the wind,
آ آ آ آ There was nothing she couldnï؟½t climb or clear,
آ آ آ آ Rich lords had vexï؟½d me, in vain, to part,
آ آ آ آ For their gold and silver, with Britomarte.

آ آ آ آ Next morn we musterï؟½d scarce half a score,
آ آ آ آ With the serving men, who were poorly armï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Five soldiers, counting myself, no more,
آ آ آ آ And a culverin, which might well have harmï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Us, had we used it, but not our foes,
آ آ آ آ When, with horses and foot, to our doors they came,
آ آ آ آ And a psalm-singer summonï؟½d us (through his nose),
آ آ آ آ And deliverï؟½d, ï؟½This, in the peopleï؟½s name,
آ آ آ آ Unto whoso holdeth this fortress here,
آ آ آ آ Surrender! or bide the siege, John Kerr.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas a mansion built in a style too new,
آ آ آ آ A castle by courtesy, he lied
آ آ آ آ Who called it a fortress, yet, ï؟½Tis true,
آ آ آ آ It had been indifferently fortified,
آ آ آ آ We were well provided with bolt and bar,
آ آ آ آ And while I hurried to place our men,
آ آ آ آ Old Miles was callï؟½d to a council of war
آ آ آ آ With Mistress Ruth and with her, and when
آ آ آ آ They had argued loudly and long, those three,
آ آ آ آ They sent, as a last resource, for me.

آ آ آ آ In the chair of state sat erect Dame Ruth;
آ آ آ آ She had cast aside her embroidery;
آ آ آ آ She had been a beauty, they say, in her youth,
آ آ آ آ There was much fierce fire in her bold black eye.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Am I deceived in you both?ï؟½ quoth she.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½If one spark of her fatherï؟½s spirit lives
آ آ آ آ In this girl here, so, this Leigh, Ralph Leigh,
آ آ آ آ Let us hear what counsel the springald gives.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then I stammerï؟½d, somewhat taken aback,
آ آ آ آ (Simon, you ale-swiller, pass the ï؟½jackï؟½).

آ آ آ آ The dame waxï؟½d hotter, ï؟½Speak out, lad, say,
آ آ آ آ Must we fall in that canting caitiffï؟½s power?
آ آ آ آ Shall we yield to a knave and a turncoat? Nay,
آ آ آ آ I had liever leap from our topmost tower.
آ آ آ آ For a while we can surely await relief;
آ آ آ آ Our walls are high and our doors are strong.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ This Kerr was indeed a canting thief,
آ آ آ آ I know not rightly, some private wrong
آ آ آ آ He had done Sir Hugh, but I know this much,
آ آ آ آ Traitor or turncoat, he sufferï؟½d as such.

آ آ آ آ Quoth Miles, ï؟½Enough! your will shall be done;
آ آ آ آ Relief may arrive by the merest chance,
آ آ آ آ But your house ere dusk will be lost and won;
آ آ آ آ They have got three pieces of ordnance.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then I cried, ï؟½Lord Guy, with four troops of horse,
آ آ آ آ Even now is biding at Westbrooke town;
آ آ آ آ If a rider could break through the rebel force,
آ آ آ آ He would bring relief ere the sun goes down;
آ آ آ آ Through the postern door could I make one dart,
آ آ آ آ I could baffle them all upon Britomarte.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Miles mutterï؟½d ï؟½Madness!ï؟½ Dame Ruth lookï؟½d grave,
آ آ آ آ Said, ï؟½True, though we cannot keep one hour
آ آ آ آ The courtyard, no, nor the stables save,
آ آ آ آ They will have to batter piecemeal the tower,
آ آ آ آ And thus - ï؟½ But suddenly she halted there.
آ آ آ آ With a shining hand on my shoulder laid
آ آ آ آ Stood Gwendoline. She had left her chair,
آ آ آ آ And, ï؟½Nay, if it needs must be done,ï؟½ she said,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Ralph Leigh will gladly do it, I ween,
آ آ آ آ For the glory of God and of Gwendoline.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ I had undertaken a heavier task
آ آ آ آ For a lighter word. I saddled with care,
آ آ آ آ Nor cumberï؟½d myself with corselet nor casque
آ آ آ آ (Being loth to burden the brave brown mare).
آ آ آ آ Young Clare kept watch on the wall, he cried,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Now, haste, Ralph! this is the time to seize;
آ آ آ آ The rebels are round us on every side,
آ آ آ آ But here they straggle by twos and threes.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then out I led her, and up I sprung,
آ آ آ آ And the postern door on its hinges swung.

آ آ آ آ I had drawn this sword, you may draw it and feel,
آ آ آ آ For this is the blade that I bore that day,
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s a notch even now on the long grey steel,
آ آ آ آ A nick that has never been raspï؟½d away.
آ آ آ آ I bowï؟½d my head and I buried my spurs,
آ آ آ آ One bound brought the gliding green beneath;
آ آ آ آ I could tell by her back-flung, flattenï؟½d ears,
آ آ آ آ She had fairly taken the bit in her teeth,
آ آ آ آ (What, Jack, have you drainï؟½d your namesake dry,
آ آ آ آ Left nothing to quench the thirst of a fly?)

آ آ آ آ These things are done, and are done with, lad,
آ آ آ آ In far less time than your talker tells;
آ آ آ آ The sward with their hoof-strokes shook like mad,
آ آ آ آ And rang with their carbines and petronels;
آ آ آ آ And they shouted, ï؟½Cross him and cut him off,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Surround him,ï؟½ ï؟½Seize him,ï؟½ ï؟½Capture the clown,
آ آ آ آ Or kill him,ï؟½ ï؟½Shall he escape to scoff
آ آ آ آ In your faces?ï؟½ ï؟½Shoot him or cut him down.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And their bullets whistled on every side;
آ آ آ آ Many were near us and more were wide.

آ آ آ آ Not a bullet told upon Britomarte;
آ آ آ آ Suddenly snorting, she launched along;
آ آ آ آ So the osprey dives where the seagulls dart,
آ آ آ آ So the falcon swoops where the kestrels throng.
آ آ آ آ And full in my front one pistol flashï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And right in my path their sergeant got.
آ آ آ آ How are jack-boots jarrï؟½d, how are stirrups clashï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ While the mare like a meteor past him shot;
آ آ آ آ But I clove his skull with a backstroke clean,
آ آ آ آ For the glory of God and of Gwendoline.

آ آ آ آ And as one whom the fierce wind storms in the face,
آ آ آ آ With spikes of hail and with splinters of rain,
آ آ آ آ I, while we fled through St. Hubertï؟½s Chase,
آ آ آ آ Bent till my cheek was amongst her mane.
آ آ آ آ To the north, full a league of the deer-park lay,
آ آ آ آ Smooth, springy turf, and she fairly flew,
آ آ آ آ And the sound of their hoof-strokes died away,
آ آ آ آ And their far shots faint in the distance grew.
آ آ آ آ Loudly I laughed, having won the start,
آ آ آ آ At the folly of following Britomarte.

آ آ آ آ They had posted a guard at the northern gate,
آ آ آ آ Some dozen of pikemen and musketeers.
آ آ آ آ To the tall park palings I turnï؟½d her straight;
آ آ آ آ She veerï؟½d in her flight as the swallow veers.
آ آ آ آ And some blew matches and some drew swords,
آ آ آ آ And one of them wildly hurlï؟½d his pike,
آ آ آ آ But she clearï؟½d by inches the oaken boards,
آ آ آ آ And she carried me yards beyond the dyke;
آ آ آ آ Then gaily over the long green down
آ آ آ آ We gallopï؟½d, heading for Westbrooke town.

آ آ آ آ The green down slopes to the great grey moor,
آ آ آ آ The grey moor sinks to the gleaming Skelt,
آ آ آ آ Sudden and sullen, and swift and sure,
آ آ آ آ The whirling water was round my belt.
آ آ آ آ She breasted the bank with a savage snort,
آ آ آ آ And a backward glance of her bloodshot eye,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Our Lady of Andoverï؟½sï؟½ flashï؟½d like thought,
آ آ آ آ And flitted St. Agathaï؟½s nunnery,
آ آ آ آ And the firs at ï؟½The Ferngroveï؟½ fled on the right,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Falconerï؟½s Towerï؟½ on the left took flight.

آ آ آ آ And over ï؟½The Ravenswoldï؟½ we raced,
آ آ آ آ We rounded the hill by ï؟½The Hermitï؟½s Wellï؟½,
آ آ آ آ We burst on the Westbrooke Bridge, ï؟½What haste?
آ آ آ آ What errand?ï؟½ shouted the sentinel.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½To Beelzebub with the Brewerï؟½s knave!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Carolus Rex and he of the Rhine!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Galloping past him, I got and gave
آ آ آ آ In the gallop password and countersign,
آ آ آ آ All soakï؟½d with water and soilï؟½d with mud,
آ آ آ آ With the sleeve of my jerkin half drenchï؟½d in blood.

آ آ آ آ Now, Heaven be praised that I found him there,
آ آ آ آ Lord Guy. He said, having heard my tale,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Leigh, let my own man look to your mare,
آ آ آ آ Rest and recruit with our wine and ale;
آ آ آ آ But first must our surgeon attend to you;
آ آ آ آ You are somewhat shrewdly stricken, no doubt.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then he snatched a horn from the wall and blew,
آ آ آ آ Making ï؟½Boot and Saddleï؟½ ring sharply out.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Have I done good service this day?ï؟½ quoth I.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Then I will ride back in your troop, Lord Guy.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ In the street I heard how the trumpets pealï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And I caught the gleam of a morion
آ آ آ آ From the window, then to the door I reelï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ I had lost more blood than I reckonï؟½d upon;
آ آ آ آ He eyed me calmly with keen grey eyes,
آ آ آ آ Stern grey eyes of a steel-blue grey,
آ آ آ آ Said, ï؟½The wilful man can never be wise,
آ آ آ آ Nathless, the wilful must have his way,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And he pourï؟½d from a flagon some fiery wine;
آ آ آ آ I drainï؟½d it, and straightway strength was mine.
آ آ آ آ .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  .
آ آ آ آ I was with them all the way on the brown,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Guy to the rescue!ï؟½ ï؟½God and the king!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ We were just in time, for the doors were down;
آ آ آ آ And didnï؟½t our sword-blades rasp and ring,
آ آ آ آ And didnï؟½t we hew and didnï؟½t we hack?
آ آ آ آ The sport scarce lasted minutes ten,
آ آ آ آ (Aye, those were the days when my beard was black;
آ آ آ آ I like to remember them now and then).
آ آ آ آ Though they fought like fiends, we were four to one,
آ آ آ آ And we captured those that refused to run.

آ آ آ آ We have not forgotten it, Cuthbert, boy!
آ آ آ آ That supper scene when the lamps were lit;
آ آ آ آ How the women (some of them) sobbï؟½d for joy,
آ آ آ آ How the soldiers drank the deeper for it;
آ آ آ آ How the dame did honours, and Gwendoline,
آ آ آ آ How grandly she glided into the hall,
آ آ آ آ How she stoopï؟½d with the grace of a girlish queen,
آ آ آ آ And kissï؟½d me gravely before them all;
آ آ آ آ And the stern Lord Guy, how gaily he laughï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Till more of his cup was spilt than quaffï؟½d.

آ آ آ آ Brown Britomarte lay dead in her straw
آ آ آ آ Next morn, we buried her, brave old girl!
آ آ آ آ John Kerr, we tried him by martial law,
آ آ آ آ And we twisted some hemp for the traitï؟½rous churl;
آ آ آ آ And she, I met her alone, said she,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½You have riskï؟½d your life, you have lost your mare,
آ آ آ آ And what can I give in return, Ralph Leigh?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ I replied, ï؟½One braid of that bright brown hair.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And with that she bowï؟½d her beautiful head,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½You can take as much as you choose,ï؟½ she said.

آ آ آ آ And I took it, it may be, more than enough,
آ آ آ آ And I shore it rudely, close to the roots.
آ آ آ آ The wine or wounds may have made me rough,
آ آ آ آ And men at the bottom are merely brutes.
آ آ آ آ Three weeks I slept at St. Hubertï؟½s Chase;
آ آ آ آ When I woke from the fever of wounds and wine,
آ آ آ آ I could scarce believe that the ghastly face
آ آ آ آ That the glass reflected was really mine.
آ آ آ آ I sought the hall, where a wedding had been,
آ آ آ آ The wedding of Guy and of Gwendoline.

آ آ آ آ The romance of a grizzled old trooperï؟½s life
آ آ آ آ May make you laugh in your sleeves: laugh out,
آ آ آ آ Lads; we have most of us seen some strife;
آ آ آ آ We have all of us had some sport, no doubt.
آ آ آ آ I have won some honour and gainï؟½d some gold,
آ آ آ آ Now that our king returns to his own;
آ آ آ آ If the pulses beat slow, if the blood runs cold,
آ آ آ آ And if friends have faded and loves have flown,
آ آ آ آ Then the greater reason is ours to drink,
آ آ آ آ And the more we swallow the less we shall think.

آ آ آ آ At the battle of Naseby, Miles was slain,
آ آ آ آ And Huntly sank from his wounds that week;
آ آ آ آ We left young Clare upon Worcester plain,
آ آ آ آ How the ï؟½Ironsideï؟½ gashï؟½d his girlish cheek.
آ آ آ آ Aye, strut, and swagger, and ruffle anew,
آ آ آ آ Gay gallants, now that the war is done!
آ آ آ آ They fought like fiends (give the fiend his due),
آ آ آ آ We fought like fops, it was thus they won.
آ آ آ آ Holdsworth is living for aught I know,
آ آ آ آ At least he was living two years ago,

آ آ آ آ And Guy, Lord Guy, so stately and stern,
آ آ آ آ He is changed, I met him at Winchester;
آ آ آ آ He has grown quite gloomy and taciturn.
آ آ آ آ Gwendoline! why do you ask for her?
آ آ آ آ Died as her mother had died before,
آ آ آ آ Died giving birth to the baby Guy!
آ آ آ آ Did my voice shake? Then am I fool the more.
آ آ آ آ Sooner or later we all must die;
آ آ آ آ But, at least, let us live while we live to-night.
آ آ آ آ The days may be dark, but the lamps are bright.

آ آ آ آ For to me the sunlight seems worn and wan:
آ آ آ آ The sun, he is losing his splendour now,
آ آ آ آ He can never shine as of old he shone
آ آ آ آ On her glorious hair and glittering brow.
آ آ آ آ Ah! those days that were, when my beard was black,
آ آ آ آ Now I have only the nights that are.
آ آ آ آ What, landlord, ho! bring in haste burnt sack,
آ آ آ آ And a flask of your fiercest usquebaugh.
آ آ آ آ You, Cuthbert! surely you know by heart
آ آ آ آ The story of her and of Britomarte.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
